title: Foundations of Organizational Behavior
simple_html:
Organizational Behavior (OB) is the study of how people think, feel, and act at work, and how that affects the organization. It blends psychology, sociology, and management to answer one practical question: why do people behave the way they do on the job, and what can managers do about it?

The core insight is that organizations are not machines; they are made of human beings whose attitudes, motivation, perceptions, and relationships drive results. A brilliant strategy fails if people are disengaged, badly led, or working against each other.

Why it matters

Managers spend most of their day dealing with people, not spreadsheets. Understanding OB helps you motivate teams, reduce turnover, resolve conflict, and design jobs people actually want to do. Decisions based on evidence about behavior beat decisions based on gut feeling or folk wisdom ("common sense" is often wrong).

The three levels of analysis

OB looks at behavior at three levels, each building on the one before:

  • Individual — personality, perception, motivation, emotions, attitudes.
  • Group/team — communication, leadership, power, conflict, teamwork.
  • Organizational — culture, structure, change.

Key takeaways

  • OB is an applied behavioral science: it uses evidence, not intuition.
  • It operates at three levels — individual, group, and organization.
  • Human factors (motivation, attitudes, leadership) explain much of why organizations succeed or fail.
  • "Evidence-based management" means testing assumptions instead of trusting common sense.
  • Good managers are, in effect, applied behavioral scientists.
intermediate_html:
Organizational Behavior (OB) is the systematic study and application of how individuals and groups act within organizations, with the goal of improving both organizational effectiveness and employee well-being. It is an interdisciplinary field, drawing on psychology (individuals), social psychology and sociology (groups and structure), and anthropology and political science (culture and power).

The three levels of analysis

The dominant organizing framework treats OB as a layered system. Inputs (personality, group structure, culture) lead to processes (motivation, communication, leadership) which produce outcomes (performance, satisfaction, turnover, citizenship behavior).

  • Individual level: personality, perception, attitudes, emotions, motivation, decision-making.
  • Group level: team dynamics, communication, leadership, power and politics, conflict.
  • Organizational level: structure, culture, change, HR systems.

This course is sequenced along exactly these levels — from individual differences and motivation, up through teams and communication, to leadership, power, culture, and change.

Evidence-based management

A central theme is replacing intuition and "half-truths" with evidence. Many widely held management beliefs (e.g., "happy workers are productive workers," "pay is the best motivator") are oversimplified or wrong. Evidence-based management means making decisions using the best available scientific evidence, sound logic, and systematically gathered organizational data, while being skeptical of fads.

Key OB outcomes

OB studies dependent variables that managers care about:

OutcomeWhat it captures
Task performanceQuality and quantity of core job duties
Organizational citizenship (OCB)Discretionary helping behavior beyond the job
Job satisfactionPositive feelings about the job
WithdrawalAbsenteeism and turnover
Counterproductive work behaviorActions that harm the organization

Worked example: applying the framework

Suppose a software firm has high turnover among engineers. A gut reaction is "pay them more." An OB lens asks at which level the problem sits. Individual: are tasks meaningful and is motivation intrinsic? Group: is the team leader supportive; is there conflict? Organizational: does the culture reward overwork? Exit-interview data might reveal that engineers leave because of micromanagement (a leadership/group issue), not pay. The intervention — manager coaching and more job autonomy — costs less and works better than across-the-board raises. This is OB in action: diagnose the right level, use data, then intervene.

How to apply it

  1. Locate the behavior at the correct level (individual, group, organizational).
  2. Gather evidence (surveys, metrics, exit interviews) rather than assuming.
  3. Choose a theory-grounded intervention matched to the cause.
  4. Measure outcomes and iterate.

The rest of the course supplies the specific theories — motivation, leadership, team development, culture — that fill in this diagnostic toolkit.
full_html:
Organizational Behavior (OB) is an applied behavioral science concerned with understanding, predicting, and influencing human behavior in organizational settings. Robbins and Judge define it as "a field of study that investigates the impact that individuals, groups, and structure have on behavior within organizations, for the purpose of applying such knowledge toward improving an organization's effectiveness" (Robbins & Judge, 2019). This foundational lesson establishes the field's scope, intellectual roots, levels of analysis, and the evidence-based orientation that underpins every later topic in the syllabus.

Intellectual roots and contributing disciplines

OB is deliberately interdisciplinary. Psychology contributes theories of individual behavior — learning, motivation, personality, perception, and emotion. Social psychology and sociology contribute group dynamics, communication, power, and organizational structure. Anthropology contributes the study of organizational culture, and political science contributes models of power and conflict. The field's modern form emerged from several streams: Frederick Taylor's scientific management (Taylor, 1911), which sought efficiency through job standardization; the human relations movement launched by the Hawthorne studies (Mayo, 1933; Roethlisberger & Dickson, 1939), which discovered that social attention and group norms shaped productivity as much as physical conditions; and later open-systems and contingency thinking (Katz & Kahn, 1966), which framed organizations as systems interacting with their environments.

The three levels of analysis

The discipline is conventionally organized into a nested, three-level model that doubles as a diagnostic map:

  • Individual level — the building block. Covers personality (e.g., the Big Five; Costa & McCrae, 1992), perception and attribution, attitudes and job satisfaction, emotions and emotional intelligence, motivation, and individual decision-making and bias.
  • Group/interpersonal level — covers team formation and development, communication, leadership, power and politics, and conflict and negotiation.
  • Organizational (systems) level — covers structure and design, organizational culture, and organizational change.

This course follows precisely this ascending order: it moves from Individual Differences (Lesson 2) and Motivation (Lessons 3-4), through Groups & Teams (Lesson 5) and Communication (Lesson 6), to Leadership (Lesson 7), Power, Politics & Conflict (Lesson 8), and finally Organizational Culture & Change (Lesson 9). Holding the three levels in mind prevents a common analytic error — attributing a structural or group problem to an individual's character.

An input-process-outcome model

A useful organizing scheme (adapted from Robbins & Judge, 2019) treats variables at each level as inputs (relatively stable: personality, group roles, culture), processes (actions and interactions: motivation, communication, leadership, conflict), and outcomes (results managers care about). Key outcome variables include task performance, organizational citizenship behavior (Organ, 1988), counterproductive work behavior, job satisfaction and organizational commitment, and withdrawal behaviors such as absenteeism and turnover. Defining the dependent variable precisely is itself a discipline: "performance" and "citizenship" respond to different drivers.

Evidence-based management

Pfeffer and Sutton (2006) argued that management is rife with "dangerous half-truths and total nonsense," and championed evidence-based management — making decisions from the best available scientific evidence, valid organizational data, and disciplined logic, while treating fads skeptically. OB's empirical posture distinguishes it from folk wisdom. For instance, the intuitive belief that "a happy worker is a productive worker" is only weakly supported; the satisfaction-performance correlation is modest, and causality may run partly in reverse (good performance breeds satisfaction). Rigorous OB resists such oversimplifications.

Worked example: diagnosing turnover

Consider a hospital losing nurses rapidly. A single-level reflex ("raise pay") may misdiagnose the cause. An OB analysis triangulates across levels: individual — are nurses experiencing low self-efficacy or emotional exhaustion?; group — is unit leadership abusive or are teams chronically understaffed?; organizational — does the culture tolerate incivility, and is scheduling rigid? Suppose engagement surveys and exit interviews (the evidence step) show that turnover clusters under specific managers and correlates with burnout, not pay. The theory-matched intervention — leadership development, workload redesign, and psychological-safety initiatives — addresses the actual drivers. Measuring post-intervention turnover closes the loop. This illustrates OB's method: locate the level, gather evidence, apply theory, intervene, and re-measure.

Nuances and debates

Several tensions animate the field. First, rigor versus relevance: academic OB prizes methodological precision, while practitioners want actionable advice, and the two can diverge. Second, the generalizability question: many findings derive from Western, often U.S., samples, raising concerns about cross-cultural validity (Hofstede, 1980) — a caution that resurfaces in the leadership lessons (e.g., the GLOBE project). Third, the levels-of-analysis problem: aggregating individual data to the group level, or inferring individual behavior from group data, risks the ecological and atomistic fallacies. Fourth, OB increasingly grapples with ethics and well-being as ends in themselves, not merely instruments for productivity.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Trusting "common sense." Many intuitive beliefs (money always motivates; conflict is always bad) are contradicted by evidence.
  • The fundamental attribution error in diagnosis. Blaming individuals for outcomes that stem from structure, incentives, or leadership (this bias is itself an OB topic).
  • Single-level thinking. Treating every problem as individual when it may be a group or system issue.
  • Chasing fads. Adopting the latest leadership or culture trend without evidence of fit.

Cross-links

This lesson is the scaffold for the entire course. The individual level expands in Individual Differences (Lesson 2) and the two motivation lessons (3-4); the group level in Groups & Teams (Lesson 5) and Communication (Lesson 6); leadership and power in Lessons 7-8; and the organizational level in Culture & Change (Lesson 9). Returning to the three-level diagnostic map after each lesson is the most reliable way to integrate the material into a coherent managerial toolkit.
